title: DAVID — The Language That Came Back

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Biblical Hebrew — a language that stopped being spoken at home for centuries, was guarded in worship, and then did the rarest thing of all: it came back.
By the Roman era, Hebrew yielded to Aramaic and Greek in daily life. It could have gone silent. Instead it survived as the language of scripture, prayer, and law — read aloud, never abandoned.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?

The proof is layered: an unbroken liturgical reading tradition, the Dead Sea Scrolls written before the vowels were marked, and the Masoretes, who added vowel points around the eighth century to fix the pronunciation they had received.
We rebuild Hebrew corpus-first: the consonantal text of the scrolls, the Masoretic vowel system of Tiberias, and comparison with Aramaic and Arabic to place sounds the script never wrote directly.
The consonants are richly attested; the Masoretic vowels record one revered reading tradition, and the older First-Temple pronunciation is reconstructed. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — Shemaʿ Yisraʾel — the opening of the Shema, with the pharyngeal ayin and the vowels the Masoretes pointed beneath the consonants.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED TEXT · Biblical Hebrew · Masoretic vocalization

beats.speech_text: Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
